title: Open a file and close it manually
prompt: Open a file called test.txt in read mode, store its content in a variable called content, print content, and close the file.
answer:
f = open("test.txt", "r")
content = f.read()
print(content)
f.close()
hint: Use open(), then read(), then close().

title: Read a file with with open
prompt: Use with open() to open test.txt in read mode, store the content in content, and print it.
answer:
with open("test.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)
hint: Use with open("test.txt", "r") as f: and then f.read().

title: Write two lines to a file
prompt: Use with open() to open test.txt in write mode and write the lines "Hello\n" and "Python\n" to it.
answer:
with open("test.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello\n")
    f.write("Python\n")
hint: Open the file with mode "w" and call write() twice.

title: Append text to a file
prompt: Use with open() to open test.txt in append mode and add the line "More text\n".
answer:
with open("test.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("More text\n")
hint: Append mode uses "a".

title: Read the whole file
prompt: Use with open() to open test.txt in read mode, store the full file in a variable called data, and print data.
answer:
with open("test.txt", "r") as f:
    data = f.read()
    print(data)
hint: Use f.read() to get the whole file as one string.

title: Read a file line by line
prompt: Use with open() to open test.txt in read mode and print each line without the ending newline character.
hint: Loop through the file and use strip() on each line.

title: Read all lines into a list
prompt: Use with open() to open test.txt in read mode, store all lines in a variable called lines with readlines(), and print lines.
answer:
with open("test.txt", "r") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

print(lines)
hint: Use f.readlines() to get a list of lines.

title: Write a list of lines
prompt:
Create lines = ["apple
", "banana
", "orange
"]. Then open fruits.txt in write mode and write the whole list to the file with writelines().
answer:
lines = ["apple
", "banana
", "orange
"]
with open("fruits.txt", "w") as f:
    f.writelines(lines)
hint: Use writelines(lines) inside a with open(..., "w") block.

title: Open a file in binary read mode
prompt: Use with open() to open photo.jpg in binary read mode, store the bytes in a variable called data, and print data.
answer:
with open("photo.jpg", "rb") as f:
    data = f.read()
    print(data)
hint: Binary read mode is "rb".

title: Create a new file with x mode
prompt: Use open() to create a new file called notes.txt with mode "x", then close it.
answer:
f = open("notes.txt", "x")
f.close()
hint: The mode "x" creates a new file and fails if it already exists.

title: Check whether a file exists
prompt: Import os. Then check whether test.txt exists. If it does, print "File exists". Otherwise print "File does not exist".
hint: Use os.path.exists("test.txt").

title: Delete a file if it exists
prompt: Import os. If test.txt exists, delete it with os.remove() and print "Deleted". Otherwise print "Not found".
hint: Check with os.path.exists() before calling os.remove().

title: Rename a file
prompt: Import os and rename old.txt to new.txt.
answer:
import os
os.rename("old.txt", "new.txt")
hint: Use os.rename(old_name, new_name).

title: Copy one file to another
prompt: Read all text from source.txt into data, then write data into target.txt.
answer:
with open("source.txt", "r") as f1:
    data = f1.read()

with open("target.txt", "w") as f2:
    f2.write(data)
hint: Read from the first file, then write the same data to the second file.

title: Read a UTF-8 file
prompt: Use with open() to open notes.txt in read mode with UTF-8 encoding and print its content.
answer:
with open("notes.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    print(f.read())
hint: Add encoding="utf-8" to open().

title: Write Unicode text to a UTF-8 file
prompt: Use with open() to open notes.txt in write mode with UTF-8 encoding and write the text "你好\nHello\n".
answer:
with open("notes.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("你好\nHello\n")
hint: Use write mode and set encoding to UTF-8.

title: Write the demo file using a variable
prompt: Create filename = "demo.txt". Then open that file in write mode with UTF-8 encoding and write "Line 1\n" and "Line 2\n".
answer:
filename = "demo.txt"
with open(filename, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("Line 1\n")
    f.write("Line 2\n")
hint: Pass the filename variable into open().

title: Read the demo file line by line
prompt: Create filename = "demo.txt". Then open it in read mode with UTF-8 encoding and print each line without the ending newline.
answer:
filename = "demo.txt"
with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())
hint: Loop over the file and use strip() on each line.

title: Read a file safely if it exists
prompt: Import os. Create filename = "abc.txt". If the file exists, open it in read mode with UTF-8 encoding and print its content. Otherwise print "File not found".
answer:
import os
filename = "abc.txt"
if os.path.exists(filename):
    with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        print(f.read())
else:
    print("File not found")
hint: Check os.path.exists(filename) before reading.

title: Print the current working directory
prompt: Import os and print the current working directory.
answer:
import os
print(os.getcwd())
hint: Use os.getcwd().

title: Append a user name to names.txt
prompt: Ask the user for their name with input("Enter your name: "). Then open names.txt in append mode with UTF-8 encoding and write the name followed by a newline.
answer:
name = input("Enter your name: ")

with open("names.txt", "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write(name + "\n")
hint: Use input() first, then append with mode "a".

title: Read and print all saved names
prompt: Open names.txt in read mode with UTF-8 encoding, print "\nAll names:", and then print the full file content.
answer:
with open("names.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    print("\nAll names:")
    print(f.read())
hint: Read the file inside the with block after printing the heading.

title: Build the beginner practice program
prompt: Ask the user for their name, append it to names.txt with UTF-8 encoding, then reopen names.txt and print "\nAll names:" followed by the full file content.
answer:
name = input("Enter your name: ")

with open("names.txt", "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write(name + "\n")

with open("names.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    print("\nAll names:")
    print(f.read())
hint: Use two with open() blocks: one for appending, one for reading.

title: Read the first line from a lines list
prompt: Open test.txt in read mode, store all lines in lines with readlines(), and print the first item in the list.
answer:
with open("test.txt", "r") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

print(lines[0])
hint: After readlines(), use index 0 for the first line.

title: Build a write then read workflow
prompt: Create filename = "demo.txt". Write "Line 1\n" and "Line 2\n" to it with UTF-8 encoding, then reopen it in read mode with UTF-8 encoding and print each line without the ending newline.
answer:
filename = "demo.txt"
with open(filename, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("Line 1\n")
    f.write("Line 2\n")

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())
hint: Write first, then open the same file again for reading.

title: Write and then append to the same file
prompt: Open test.txt in write mode and write "Hello\n". Then open the same file in append mode and add "More text\n".
answer:
with open("test.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello\n")

with open("test.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("More text\n")
hint: Use one with block for mode "w" and another for mode "a".

title: Write a fruit list and read it back
prompt:
Create lines = ["apple
", "banana
", "orange
"]. Write it to fruits.txt with writelines(), then reopen fruits.txt in read mode and print the full content.
answer:
lines = ["apple\n", "banana\n", "orange\n"]
with open("fruits.txt", "w") as f:
    f.writelines(lines)

with open("fruits.txt", "r") as f:
    print(f.read())
hint: Use writelines() first, then reopen the file and use read().
